questions.explanation: @PermitAll opens the endpoint to all callers with no authentication required. @RolesAllowed({'admin','user'}) requires a valid, cryptographically signed JWT token whose groups claim contains 'admin' or 'user'. The injected JsonWebToken gives access to token claims like subject.

questions.question: What is Arquillian?
options: A) A build tool similar to Maven for Jakarta EE projects
B) A container-agnostic integration testing framework for Jakarta EE
C) A monitoring tool for production application servers
D) A code generation tool for JPA entities
questions.explanation: Arquillian is a container-agnostic integration testing framework for Jakarta EE that minimizes the burden of managing containers and deployments, letting developers focus on writing tests.

questions.question: Which class/API in Arquillian is used to declaratively define the deployment archive for a test?
options: A) TestArchive
B) DeploymentBuilder
C) ShrinkWrap
D) ArtifactFactory
questions.explanation: ShrinkWrap is the API used to declaratively define a deployment archive. Static methods annotated with @Deployment return a ShrinkWrap archive for Arquillian to deploy.

questions.question: What is Payara Micro, used in the Arquillian test execution example?
options: A) A JavaScript testing framework
B) A smaller version of Payara Server designed specifically for microservices and containerized environments
C) A Maven plugin for running integration tests
D) A monitoring dashboard for Jakarta EE applications
questions.explanation: Payara Micro is a lightweight version of Payara Server, designed specifically for microservices and containerized environments, used in the Arquillian example for embedded testing.

questions.question: In Arquillian, what does 'embedded' execution mode mean?
options: A) The container runs in a separate JVM on a remote server
B) Arquillian manages the container lifecycle, but it runs in a separate JVM
C) The container runs inside the same JVM as the test and is managed by Arquillian
D) Tests are executed inside the web browser
questions.explanation: In embedded mode, the container resides in the same JVM as the test execution program and is managed by Arquillian.

questions.question: Which testing frameworks does Arquillian provide test launchers for?
options: A) Mocha and Jasmine
B) JUnit and TestNG
C) PyTest and unittest
D) Cucumber and Selenium
questions.explanation: Arquillian supplies test launchers for the two most popular Java testing choices: JUnit and TestNG.

questions.question: How does Arquillian support dependency injection in test classes?
options: A) It does not — test classes must create all dependencies manually
B) It enriches test classes by supporting @Inject, @Resource, @EJB, and @PersistenceContext, just like production code
C) It uses Spring's @Autowired annotation
D) Only constructor injection is supported
questions.explanation: Arquillian enriches tests to support standard Jakarta EE injection: @Inject for CDI, @Resource for resources, @EJB for session beans, and @PersistenceContext for JPA — making tests feel like production code.

questions.question: What are the Arquillian extensions Drone and Graphene used for?
options: A) Performance and load testing of Jakarta EE applications
B) Web browser automation testing linked to Selenium WebDriver
C) Testing JMS message flows
D) Mocking CDI beans in unit tests
questions.explanation: Drone and Graphene are Arquillian extensions linked to Selenium's WebDriver API, enabling automated web browser control for end-to-end testing of web UIs.

options: A) @Deployment marks the class as a deployable artifact; ShrinkWrap is a build tool that compiles Java code

B) @Deployment marks a static method that builds the test archive (containing MyService and a beans.xml); Arquillian deploys this archive to the container before running tests, and @Inject works inside the test class
C) @Deployment tells Arquillian to skip container setup and run tests directly in the JVM
D) ShrinkWrap replaces Maven and resolves dependencies at test time
questions.explanation: @ExtendWith(ArquillianExtension.class) hooks Arquillian into JUnit 5. @Deployment builds the micro-archive (WAR with MyService + beans.xml) that Arquillian deploys to the container. @Inject then works in the test class just as in production code.

questions.question:
Looking at the Arquillian test execution flow with Payara Micro, what happens for EACH test class?

Unit test class
  → for each unit test class:
    1. Start Payara Micro and deploy archive
    2. Run test methods
    3. Stop Payara Micro
options: A) Payara Micro starts once at the beginning and stays running for all test classes

B) For each test class, Payara Micro starts fresh (deploying the archive), all test methods in that class run inside the container, then Payara Micro stops — giving each class a clean container environment
C) Payara Micro runs only the @Deployment method, not the @Test methods
D) Tests run outside the container; only the @Deployment archive is deployed to Payara Micro
questions.explanation: In the embedded Arquillian + Payara Micro setup, the lifecycle is per test class: start container → deploy → run all @Test methods → stop. This ensures test isolation but adds startup overhead per class.

questions.question: What is Eclipse MicroProfile?
options: A) A lightweight Java IDE for microservices development
B) A community and open source 'umbrella' specification providing APIs focused on cloud-native application development
C) A Docker-based deployment framework for Jakarta EE
D) A monitoring agent for application servers
questions.explanation: MicroProfile is an umbrella specification from the Eclipse Foundation (started September 2016) providing a set of APIs focused on cloud-native (microservices, cloud) application development.

questions.question: What does MicroProfile Config provide?
options: A) Runtime monitoring of application performance metrics
B) A flexible and extensible application configuration mechanism supporting system properties, environment variables, and properties files
C) A way to configure WebSocket endpoints
D) Automatic generation of REST client stubs
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Config defines a flexible configuration mechanism. Mandatory sources include system properties, environment variables, and META-INF/microprofile-config.properties files.

questions.question: What are the three types of health checks in MicroProfile Health?
options: A) CPU, Memory, Disk
B) Readiness, Liveness, Startup
C) Active, Passive, Scheduled
D) HTTP, JMX, Log
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Health defines three health check types: readiness (is the app ready to handle requests?), liveness (is the app alive?), and startup (has the app finished starting?).

questions.question: What does MicroProfile OpenAPI provide?
options: A) An implementation of the OpenID Connect standard
B) Java interfaces and programming models to natively produce OpenAPI v3 compliant documents for JAX-RS REST services
C) A tool to generate JPA entities from an OpenAPI spec
D) An open-source API gateway for routing requests
questions.explanation: MicroProfile OpenAPI provides a set of Java interfaces enabling developers to produce OpenAPI v3 compliant documents for their JAX-RS REST services, exposed at the /openapi endpoint.

questions.question: What does MicroProfile Fault Tolerance allow developers to do?
options: A) Automatically fix bugs in production code
B) Define resilience patterns like timeouts, retries, fallbacks, and circuit breakers on method calls
C) Monitor and alert on application faults
D) Roll back database transactions on any exception
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Fault Tolerance defines annotations like @Timeout, @Retry, @Fallback, and @CircuitBreaker to make microservices resilient to failures.

questions.question: What does MicroProfile JWT RBAC enable?
options: A) JSON-based configuration files for role definitions
B) Role-based access control for microservice endpoints using OpenID Connect and JSON Web Tokens (JWT)
C) Automatic generation of JWT tokens from JPA entities
D) Bidirectional communication using JWT-secured WebSockets
questions.explanation: MicroProfile JWT RBAC enables role-based securing of microservice endpoints. A caller authenticates using a cryptographically signed JWT token specifying the user and their groups.

questions.question: What does MicroProfile Rest Client provide?
options: A) A server-side REST framework replacing JAX-RS
B) A typed, annotation-driven approach to calling RESTful services over HTTP
C) A GraphQL client for microservices
D) A tool to document REST APIs automatically
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Rest Client describes a typed approach to calling RESTful services over HTTP, allowing you to define an interface and inject it as a REST client.

questions.question: MicroProfile OpenTelemetry is based on which CNCF project, and what does it primarily help with?
options: A) Prometheus — collecting application metrics
B) OpenTelemetry — enabling REST services to participate in distributed tracing, metrics, and logging
C) Jaeger — providing a UI for viewing application logs
D) Grafana — creating dashboards for application data
questions.explanation: MicroProfile OpenTelemetry is based on the CNCF OpenTelemetry project, defining an API enabling REST services to participate in a distributed tracing environment, with data sent to backends like Jaeger or Zipkin.

questions.question: What is a 'cloud-native application'?
options: A) An application that can only run on AWS or Azure
B) An application specifically designed for cloud environments, leveraging microservices architecture, containers, cloud services, and DevOps practices
C) Any Java EE application deployed to a cloud server
D) An application that uses cloud storage instead of a local database
questions.explanation: A cloud-native application is designed for cloud environments: it follows microservices architecture, uses containers (Docker/Kubernetes), relies on cloud services, and emphasizes CI/CD and dynamic resource management.

questions.question: Which MicroProfile specification is responsible for exposing application metrics in JSON or Prometheus format via an HTTP endpoint?
options: A) MicroProfile Health
B) MicroProfile OpenTelemetry
C) MicroProfile Metrics
D) MicroProfile OpenAPI
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Metrics collects various metrics over the application lifetime and exposes them via REST over HTTP in JSON or Prometheus format — consumed by tools like Prometheus and displayed in Grafana.

questions.question: What is the key difference between Jakarta EE and Eclipse MicroProfile in terms of application style?
options: A) Jakarta EE is open source; MicroProfile is commercial
B) Jakarta EE targets traditional monolithic enterprise apps (.war, .ear), while MicroProfile focuses on cloud-native microservices
C) Jakarta EE only supports SOAP; MicroProfile only supports REST
D) MicroProfile is a subset of Jakarta EE with the same set of specifications
questions.explanation: Jakarta EE targets both monolithic and web-based enterprise applications, while MicroProfile focuses specifically on cloud-native microservices. They share values like standardization and openness, and often complement each other.

questions.question: In MicroProfile Config, how would you retrieve a configuration value programmatically (not via injection)?
options: A) System.getenv("my.property")
B) Config config = ConfigProvider.getConfig(); String val = config.getValue("my.property", String.class);
C) Properties p = new Properties(); p.load(...); p.getProperty("my.property");
D) @ConfigProperty(name="my.property") private String val;
questions.explanation: Programmatic access uses ConfigProvider.getConfig() to get a Config instance, then config.getValue() to retrieve a typed value. @ConfigProperty is the injection-based approach.

options: A) @RegisterRestClient turns SwapiService into a JMS message producer; @RestClient injects a queue

B) @RegisterRestClient marks the interface so MicroProfile generates a working HTTP client implementation; @Inject @RestClient injects that generated implementation — calling swapiService.getCharacter('1') makes a real GET HTTP call to the configured URL
C) @RegisterRestClient creates a mock of SwapiService for testing only
D) The interface must be a class; interfaces cannot be registered as Rest Clients
questions.explanation: @RegisterRestClient tells MicroProfile Rest Client to generate a proxy implementation for this interface. The base URL is configured in microprofile-config.properties. @Inject @RestClient injects the proxy — method calls on it become real HTTP requests.

options: A) @Timeout logs timing; @Retry logs retries; @Fallback logs failures
B) @Timeout aborts the call if it takes too long; @Retry automatically retries the call on failure; @Fallback calls calculateAmountFallback() if all retries are exhausted or a timeout occurs
C) @Retry runs the method in parallel threads; @Timeout stops parallel threads; @Fallback merges results
D) All three annotations do the same thing — they are aliases for each other
questions.explanation: @Timeout sets a maximum execution time and aborts if exceeded. @Retry retries the method invocation on failure. @Fallback specifies a fallback method (calculateAmountFallback) to call when all retry attempts fail or a non-retryable error occurs — combining three resilience patterns on a single method.

options: A) The /health endpoint returns HTTP 200 with status 'UP' regardless of disk space
B) The /health endpoint returns HTTP 503 with status 'DOWN' and the remaining disk space in MB, because status(false) marks the check as DOWN
C) The method throws an ArithmeticException when freeSpace is 0
D) @Liveness means this check only runs at application startup
questions.explanation: .status(freeSpace > 100) sets status to UP if freeSpace > 100, DOWN otherwise. When DOWN, the /health endpoint returns HTTP 503 Service Unavailable with a JSON body showing status:'DOWN' and the remainingSpace data. @Liveness marks it as a liveness probe (is the app alive?).
